critic_raw: I want to probe the 'coverage' secondary metric more carefully. The design says coverage measures 'percentage of queries for which the model returns candidates (vs. no match in product area/version constraints).' If the dense model returns fewer unique candidates (due to embedding space clustering), some queries may receive fewer than 5 unique relevant candidates and the NDCG@5 would be computed over a smaller actual list. The metric may penalize dense retrieval for being more selective. Additionally, the design uses 'bootstrap resampling of test queries' for confidence intervals. If queries are nested within support sessions (multiple queries from the same session), bootstrap resampling of individual queries may underestimate variance.
defender_raw: Round 1: On coverage and candidate selectivity: the design explicitly includes coverage as a metric precisely to capture this — if dense retrieval returns fewer candidates, coverage would be lower, signaling a deployment concern. NDCG@5 computed on smaller candidate sets (fewer than 5 returned) can be handled by treating missing positions as zero relevance, which is standard. The concern is valid for deployment but is monitored by the coverage metric. On bootstrap query sampling and session nesting: support queries are semi-independent (different customers, different agents), so session nesting is less of a concern than for user-study data. If session nesting is detected, block bootstrapping at the session level would be more appropriate — this is a valid statistical refinement.

rounds.critic_followup: On coverage: if the dense model's coverage is lower (returns fewer candidates for more queries), the effective test set for NDCG evaluation differs between models. Comparing NDCG@5 on a subset of queries where dense retrieval finds candidates vs. NDCG@5 on the full query set (where BM25 always finds candidates) is not an apples-to-apples comparison.
rounds.defender_followup: Both models are evaluated on the full query set. For queries where the dense model returns no candidates (zero coverage), NDCG@5 = 0 for that query. This correctly penalizes the dense model for low coverage. The coverage metric is reported separately to characterize when and why NDCG@5 = 0. The BM25 baseline's lexical retrieval naturally returns results for any query with at least one keyword match in the product area, so its coverage would typically be near 100%. If dense retrieval's coverage is substantially lower, this is an honest result that the NDCG@5 primary metric captures via zero scores.

rounds.critic_followup: Acknowledged on zero-score handling. On the BM25 re-run at test evaluation: the design says 'BM25 baseline is re-run on test data using tuned parameters to verify it does not degrade on held-out data.' This is a validation step. But does the design permit re-tuning BM25 parameters if test performance degrades?
rounds.defender_followup: The BM25 re-run is a validation check only — it verifies that the tuned BM25 parameters generalize. If test performance degrades significantly, this would be flagged as a finding (distribution shift or concept drift) not as an opportunity to re-tune. Re-tuning on test performance would invalidate the held-out evaluation. The design's intent is clear: test set is touched once for final evaluation; any degradation is a finding to report. This is standard ML experiment discipline.

adjudication_raw: Two rounds on coverage and NDCG comparison fairness, and BM25 re-run interpretation. Both resolved: zero-NDCG correctly penalizes no-candidate queries; BM25 re-run is a validation check not re-tuning opportunity. Verdict: defense_wins.
